analogy: Think of "On Equality" through the chapter's own opening images: 3 Equality in Indian Democracy Teachers note This Unit introduces the learner to the critical role of equality in democracy, with specific reference to India. The Constitution of India guarantees equality to all citizens. Despite this, the daily lives of people in India are far... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 4 Social and Political Life, CHAPTER, 6 Social and Political Life, 8 Social and Political Life. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
On Equality is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 3 Equality in Indian Democracy Teachers note This Unit introduces the learner to the critical role of equality in democracy, with specific reference to India.

The text explains that the Constitution of India guarantees equality to all citizens.

The text explains that despite this, the daily lives of people in India are far from equal.

The text explains that earlier discussions on equality in Civics textbooks most often reiterated certain provisions of the Constitution without adequately considering the reality of these in peoples lives.

The text explains that it discusses the need for equality through highlighting the inequalities that continue to be practised and experienced by various communities.

The text explains that the governments role in passing laws and instituting policies is discussed to show that commitment to the alleviation of existing inequalities is a major part of the work that governments undertake.

The text explains that the chapter also briefly introduces an issue of inequality in the United States of America to highlight that this is a global phenomenon and a feature of many democratic countries.

The text explains that this chapter aims to help the learner understand that equality and democracy are dynamic and not static concepts.

The text explains that this dynamism is reflected in the governments passing of new laws and programmes, and in peoples movements around various social and economic issues.

The text explains that local situations should be used as a practical reference to make the discussion on underlying concepts more relevant and appropriate.

The text explains that a discussion on equality in the classroom requires empathy as well as a firm commitment on the teachers part to ensuring that the dignity of all learners be respected.

The text explains that 4 Social and Political Life CHAPTER On Equality Equality in Indian democracy The Indian Constitution recognises every person as equal.

The text explains that this means that every individual in the country, including male and female persons from all castes, religions, tribes, educational and economic backgrounds are recognised as equal.

The text explains that this is not to say that inequality ceases to exist.

The text explains that but atleast, in democratic India, the principle of the equality of all persons is recognised.

The text explains that while earlier no law existed to protect people from discrimination and ill-treatment, now there are several that work to see that people are treated with dignity and as equals.

The text explains that this recognition of equality includes some of the following provisions in the Constitution: first that every person is equal before the law.

The text explains that while government programmes play an important role in increasing equality of opportunity, there is much that still needs to be done.

The text explains that one of the main reasons for this is that attitudes change very slowly.

The text explains that establishing equality in a democratic society is a continuous struggle and one in which individuals as well as various communities in India contribute to and you will read more about this in this book.

The text explains that issues of equality in other democracies You are probably wondering whether India is the only democratic country in which there is inequality and Children being served their midday meal at a government school in Uttarakhand.

The text explains that find out about one government scheme in your area.

The text explains that this, despite the fact that there was a movement in the late 1950s to push for equal rights for African Americans.

The text explains that her refusal that day started a huge agitation against the unequal ways in which AfricanAmericans were treated and which came to be known as the Civil Rights Movement.

The text explains that the Civil Rights Act of 1964 prohibited discrimination on the basis of race, religion or national origin.

The text explains that ambedkar Chapter 1: On Equality Rosa Parks, an African American woman, changed the course of American history with one defiant act.

The text explains that most African-American children can only afford to attend government schools that have fewer facilities and poorly qualified teachers as compared to white students who either go to private schools or live in areas where the government schools are as highly rated as private schools.

The text explains that as you read these chapters, think about whether the equality of all persons and their being able to maintain their dignity is upheld.

The text explains that excerpt from Article 15 of the Indian Constitution

The text explains that according to the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016, persons with disabilities have equal rights, and that the government should make possible their full participation in society.

The text explains that the government has to provide free education and integrate children with disabilities into mainstream schools.

The text explains that it means that all adult (those who are 18 and above) citizens have the right to vote irrespective of their social or economic backgrounds.

The text explains that constitution: This is a document that lays down the basic rules and regulations for people and the government in the country to follow.

The text explains that civil Rights Movement: A movement that began in USA in 1950s in which AfricanAmerican people demanded equal rights and an end to racial discrimination. http://disabilityaffairs.gov.in Chapter 1: On Equality

The text explains that this law also states that all public places including buildings, schools, etc., should be accessible and provided with ramps.

The text explains that look at the photograph and think about the boy who is being carried down the stairs.

The text explains that do you think the above law is being implemented in his case?

The text explains that what needs to be done to make the building more accessible for him?

The text explains that how would his being carried down the stairs affect his dignity as well as his safety?

The text explains that glossary Universal adult franchise: This is a very important aspect of democratic societies.

The text explains that dignity: This refers to thinking of oneself and other persons as worthy of respect.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II, Chapter 1: On Equality

Do not add outside examples to On Equality; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Use 4 Social and Political Life and CHAPTER to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
